data.title: Penguin solutions raises FY2025 revenue growth outlook to 17% amid Q2 revenue growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Penguin Solutions (PENG) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Mark Adams highlighted a 28% year-over-year revenue growth to $366 million for Q2 2025, with non-GAAP earnings per share reaching $0.52, a 97% increase. He emphasized the company's transformation into a leader in high-performance enterprise infrastructure solutions, focusing on AI adoption.
  • Adams discussed advancements in the Advanced Computing segment, which generated $200 million in revenue, up 42% year-over-year, driven by growth in hyperscalers, federal, and other sectors. Integrated Memory revenue rose 26% to $105 million, with growing demand for high-reliability memory. Optimized LED remained flat at $60 million but saw improved margins.
  • Adams highlighted strategic initiatives, including the launch of ICE ClusterWare AIM Service for AI infrastructure optimization and progress in partnerships with SK Telecom and Dell to expand market opportunities.
  • CFO Nate Olmstead noted improved operating income margins and emphasized disciplined expense management, with operating expenses down year-over-year and sequentially.

Outlook

  • Adams announced an updated full-year fiscal 2025 revenue growth forecast, raising the midpoint from 15% to 17%. By segment, Advanced Computing is expected to grow between 15% and 25%, Integrated Memory between 20% and 30%, and LED revenue is expected to remain flat.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin guidance was set at 31%, plus or minus 1 percentage point. Full-year non-GAAP diluted EPS is projected at $1.60, plus or minus $0.10.
  • Adams noted that revenue and profits will be weighted more heavily in the first half due to significant shipments in Advanced Computing.

Financial Results

  • The company reported $366 million in revenue, up 28% year-over-year. Advanced Computing contributed $200 million, Integrated Memory $105 million, and Optimized LED $60 million.
  • Non-GAAP operating income reached $49 million, an 85% increase from the prior year, with non-GAAP gross margins at 30.8%. Adjusted EBITDA was $54 million, up 61% year-over-year.
  • Cash and cash equivalents totaled $647 million, a significant increase from the prior quarter, driven by operating cash flow and disciplined inventory management.

Q&A

  • Denis Pyatchanin (Stifel) inquired about revenue drivers in the second half. Adams noted that growth in verticals such as federal, financial, and energy sectors would complement the first half's hyperscaler strength.
  • Samik Chatterjee (JPMorgan) asked about tariff impacts on manufacturing. Adams explained that tariffs are already embedded in their numbers and highlighted the resilience of the LED segment's profitability.
  • Alek Valero (Loop Capital) asked about SK Telecom's partnership. Adams expressed optimism about collaborative opportunities in AI strategy and data center infrastructure.
  • Nick Doyle (Needham & Company) asked about the multi-tenant support in ICE ClusterWare. Adams explained its relevance to cloud service providers and private clouds.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism about the company's growth prospects, with questions focused on demand trends, partnerships, and market dynamics.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, particularly about the updated revenue outlook and strategic initiatives. Adams emphasized the company's unique value proposition and growth opportunities.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management displayed increased confidence in the company's market position, particularly in AI infrastructure.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue grew from $341 million in Q1 to $366 million in Q2, with Advanced Computing and Integrated Memory segments contributing significantly to this growth.
  • Non-GAAP EPS increased from $0.49 in Q1 to $0.52 in Q2.
  • Revenue guidance was raised from 15% to 17%, reflecting a more optimistic outlook based on strong first-half performance.
  • Management's tone was more bullish, with a focus on long-term growth potential and strategic partnerships.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged risks related to supply chain constraints, particularly in Advanced Computing and LED segments.
  • Analysts raised concerns about potential macroeconomic impacts on customer engagement and infrastructure build-outs.
  • Management highlighted their conservative approach to forecasting and inventory management to mitigate these risks.

Final Takeaway

Penguin Solutions delivered strong Q2 results, driven by robust growth in Advanced Computing and Integrated Memory segments. The company raised its fiscal 2025 revenue growth outlook to 17%, reflecting confidence in its pipeline and partnerships. Strategic initiatives in AI infrastructure solutions and collaborations with SK Telecom and Dell are expected to support long-term growth. Despite macroeconomic and supply chain challenges, management remains focused on disciplined execution and innovation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil falls after Trump's tariff news on worries over bearish impact on oil demand
data.description:
Crude oil futures turned lower after initially rising in post-settlement trading Wednesday as President Trump unveiled a sweeping plan for reciprocal tariffs on U.S. trading partners.

The front-month May contract on Nymex recently traded down 2.8% to $69.69/bbl, after posting modest gains in the regular session.

The president said the U.S. will impose a 10% across-the-board tariff on all imports, plus reciprocal levies on ~60 countries with the largest trade deficits with the U.S., including a 34% levy on goods from China, a 24% rate on Japanese goods, and 20% on products from the European Union.

A chart Trump displayed during his announcement did not detail tariffs on Canada and Mexico, but USMCA-compliant goods from Canada and Mexico, including oil, apparently would remain exempt from the tariffs, Reuters reported; Canada supplies ~4M bbl/day of its crude oil to the U.S.

"Oil is selling off a little on the news, and it could introduce some additional trade and economic uncertainties, but I think people were worried it would be more extreme," Josh Young, chief investment officer at Bison Interests, said following Trump's tariff announcement.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery settled +0.7% to $71.71/bbl, front-month June Brent crude (CO1:COM) closed +0.6% to $74.95/bbl, the best settlement value for both benchmarks since February 20.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) rebounded ahead of Thursday's weekly storage report from the EIA, which is expected to show a 25B cf injection into storage in a third consecutive weekly build, according to a WSJ survey; the May front-month contract ended +2.6% to $4.055/MMBtu.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

Crude futures gained in regular trading, despite a surprise 6.2M-barrel build in U.S. crude stocks to 439.8M barrels in the week ended March 28, ~4% below the five-year average for the time of year, the Energy Information Administration reported; analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal had expected crude stockpiles to fall by 700K barrels.

U.S. crude oil production was flat at 13.58M bbl/day, crude imports rose by 271K bbl/day to 6.5M bbl/day, exports fell by 728K bbl/day to 3.9M bbl/day, and refinery capacity use fell to 86% from 87% in the previous week, according to the EIA.

"Every crude oil input was a bearish number in this week's report," Mizuho analyst Robert Yawger said, as U.S. crude imports rose, exports fell, and refineries processed less crude than the week before.

"The report was bearish... but the market took it as neutral, as the crude build is driven by a sharp increase in Canadian crude imports, likely ahead of the fear of the introduction of new tariffs," UBS analyst Giovanni Staunovo said.

data.title: Meta's Zuckerberg lobbying President Trump to escape upcoming FTC trial - WSJ
data.description:
Meta's (NASDAQ:META) top boss, Mark Zuckerberg, is reportedly lobbying President Donald Trump and White House officials to agree to a settlement that would prevent his company from facing an antitrust trial later this month.

According to a Wall Street Journal report citing people familiar with the matter, representatives of the social media and tech giant met with Trump and his senior advisers ahead of a Federal Trade Commission trial later this month that could force the company to undo its acquisition of WhatsApp and Instagram.

The terms of a potential settlement weren't immediately clear, the WSJ report said.

People told the WSJ that Zuckerberg was at the White House on Wednesday, his third visit during Trump's presidency. According to some people, a few of Trump's aides have "grown frustrated at the company's lobbying strategy" and claim it has been too aggressive.

"We regularly meet with policymakers to discuss issues impacting competitiveness, national security and economic growth," Meta spokesperson Andy Stone reportedly told WSJ.

The report said the Trump administration has not decided whether it will settle with the company over accusations that it bought Instagram and WhatsApp to defeat competition.

"It is unusual for companies involved in big antitrust lawsuits to go to the White House, but it has happened before," Jon Leibowitz, a former chairman of the FTC during the Obama administration, told WSJ.

The FTC suit alleges Facebook "is illegally maintaining its personal social networking monopoly through a years-long course of anticompetitive conduct."

The complaint said Facebook put together a deliberate strategy to "eliminate threats to its monopoly." Meta has argued, saying that the FTC cleared the deals more than a decade ago, and it now competes with YouTube (GOOG) (GOOGL), X, and iMessage (AAPL).

data.title: Pharmaceuticals excluded from reciprocal tariffs announced by Trump
data.description:
Pharma and biotech companies are breathing a sigh of relief after the reciprocal tariffs announced by President Donald Trump Wednesday afternoon excludes pharmaceuticals. 

A White House fact sheet mentions that pharmaceuticals are exempt. However, the president had recently mentioned his desire to include tariffs on them. 

Speaking at an event in the White House Rose Garden, Trump expressed concern that many drugs are produced outside the U.S. and supply issues could crop up in the event of wars. 

In his comments, Trump noted that several large drugmakers -- Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Eli Lilly (LLY), and Merck (MRK) -- have recently made announcements of making manufacturing investments in the U.S.

Most active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) produced overseas are used in the manufacture of generic drugs, which account for 9 out of 10 prescriptions filled in the U.S.

That means generic drugmakers, such as Viatris (NASDAQ:VTRS) and Teva Pharmaceutical Industries (NYSE:TEVA), could see significant impact if foreign-made pharmaceuticals are eventually subject to tariffs.

India, China, and the European Union produce the vast majority of APIs and also manufacture a significant amount of finished products.

If tariffs are eventually extended to pharmaceuticals, the country tariffs are as follows: India (26%); China (34%); and European Union (20%).

Trump noted that the reciprocal tariffs on the trio are much less than the tariffs they impose on American goods: India (52%); China (67%); and European Union (39%).

According to the U.S. Pharmacopeia, as of 2021, India manufactured nearly half -- 48% -- of the APIs made into drugs consumed in the U.S. Europe made 22%, and China, 13%. Just 10% of APIs in 2021 were manufactured in the U.S.

A 2022 evaluation of the top 100 Medicare Part D drugs based on spend from PharmacyChecker Research found that 32 were finished in the U.S. and another 67 were finished in countries including European Union countries, Canada, Japan, Singapore, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Fed Gov. Kugler: Inflation expectations may cause real inflation to go up
data.description:
As the Federal Reserve has seen several measures of inflation expectations rise, new and imposed tariffs could slow down growth, said Governor Adriana D. Kugler, member of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System.

At The Griswold Center for Economic Policy Studies and Julis-Rabinowitz Center for Public Policy and Finance 2025 Public Talk, Gov. Kugler said that the anticipatory nature of the recent increase in short-run expectations may push businesses to pass along higher costs to consumers.

“Firms are already reporting not only higher costs, but also expectations of higher costs,” she said. “I take some comfort from the much smaller increases in longer-term expectations as measured by the Philadelphia Fed's Survey of Professional Forecasters, as well as the stability of longer-term measures of what we call inflation compensation.”

Friday's survey on longer-term inflation expectations from the University of Michigan survey was the highest since February 1993, but the recent spike in short-term inflation expectations, according to the survey by the University of Michigan, appears to be mostly "anticipatory."

“We have also seen measures of economic policy uncertainty increase, and there is evidence that policy uncertainty and inflation uncertainty correlate over time,” she said. “One possibility is that policy uncertainty may be contributing to a rise in inflation expectations as well as to uncertainty about future inflation.”

In addition, recent disinflation – after the decline from its peak in 2022 – has been slower and could indicate that the FOMC’s 2% target may have stalled.

Core PCE inflation was 2.8% in the 12 months that ended in February, “which puts us back at the same level seen in the last quarter of 2024,” she pointed out.

“While I do not discount price pressures in non-market services, which remain elevated, the acceleration in market-based services in February from an estimated 3.1% to 3.5% is also not welcome, given that this category often provides a better signal of inflationary pressures across all services.”

She concluded that due to the lack of inflationary progress, the recent increases in inflation expectations, and the risk of tariff impositions, she supported the FOMC’s March meeting decision to leave the target range for the federal funds rate unchanged.

More on U.S. economy:

  • US economy grows slightly in recent weeks: Fed's Beige Book report
  • US economy on path to 2% inflation, but policy should stay where it is, Atlanta Fed's Bostic says
  • U.S. economy ‘can’t survive’ under Trump uncertainty – Avenue Capital Group

data.title: Stocks of U.S. manufacturers slide after Trump's tariff plans
data.description:
Stocks of U.S. manufacturers including GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) and Eaton (NYSE:ETN) fell in extended trading as investors discounted for the possible effects of President Trump’s tariffs on imports.

The president on Wednesday launched his most aggressive move yet against what he sees as an imbalanced global trade system, taking aim at U.S. trading partners across the board.

Trump announced plans to impose at least a 10% tariff on all countries exporting goods to the United States. In addition, roughly 60 nations with the largest trade deficits with the U.S. will face even steeper tariffs.

Company (Ticker) After-hours move as of 5:44 p.m. ET

GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) -2.8%

RTX (NYSE:RTX) -2.5%

Boeing (NYSE:BA) -2.2%

Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT) -1.1%

Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT) -2.6%

Honeywell International (NASDAQ:HON) -0.5%

Deere (NYSE:DE) -2%

Eaton (NYSE:ETN) -3.3%

3M (NYSE:MMM) -0.8%

Parker-Hannifin (NYSE:PH) -1.8%

data.title: Gold gains after-hours as Trump's reciprocal tariffs add to haven demand
data.description:
Gold prices added to gains after regular trading Wednesday, sparked by further safe-haven inflows after President Trump announced a sweeping plan to institute reciprocal tariffs that are roughly half of the administration's calculation of tariffs on other nations.

President Trump said the U.S. will impose a 10% across-the-board tariff on all imports, plus reciprocal levies on many countries; examples include China, which will be charged tariffs of 34%, the European Union will be charged 20%, and Vietnam 46%.

Most-active Comex gold for June delivery currently is up 1.4% at $3,190.30/oz, after trading 0.6% higher before the president's Rose Garden announcement started.

"The reciprocal tariffs are much more aggressive than expected, which should lead to asset market selloffs and a lower dollar," metals trader Tai Wong told Reuters.

"Gold's prospects are excellent here with $3,200 the new short-term target," Wong added. "There are plenty of unanswered questions and the sense that many things might be negotiable will make markets very volatile in the short term."

In regular trading, front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for April delivery posted another new record high settlement, +0.7% to $3,139.90/oz, its fourth gain in the past five sessions, while front-month April silver (XAGUSD:CUR) closed +1% to $34.499/oz, snapping a three-day losing streak.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

"We expect gold has further room to run, especially when the U.S. Fed cuts rates again, and anticipate significant volatility as global markets remain on edge," analysts at Fitch's BMI research unit said. "Gold will once again be an outperformer within the wider metals complex, a scenario that would be put into limbo only if the Fed proceeds to raise interest rates instead."

Average gold price levels should trend upward over the next 6-9 months, with a potential to break out to $3,300-$3,400/oz, according to strategists at State Street Global Advisors SPDR, pointing to tailwinds including rebounding investor demand, rising U.S. recession and stagflation risks, prevailing geopolitical uncertainty and ongoing emerging-market central bank purchases.

"The key bullish theme for the 2025 gold trade seems to be financial flows catching-up to robust physical demand in 2023-2024," State Street said.

data.title: Chinese online retailers retreat as Trump ends de minimis exemption
data.description:
Shares of PDD Holdings (NASDAQ:PDD), the parent company of online marketplace Temu, JD.com (JD), and Alibaba (BABA) fell sharply in late trading after President Trump confirmed he was eliminating the de minimis treatment for low-value imports from China through an Executive Order.

According to the White House, U.S. Customs and Border Protection processes over 4 million de minimis shipments into the U.S. each day.

The de minimis exemption allows goods with a value of $800 or less to enter the U.S. duty-free. Temu, SHEIN, AliExpress and other Chinese retailers exploit this exemption to ship individual packages to customers out of the country without paying import tariffs and precludes these retailers from operating fulfillment centers in the U.S. similar to Amazon (AMZN) and Walmart (WMT).

The order will take effect on May 2 at 12:01am EDT.

The Executive Order states that imported goods sent through means other than the international postal network that are valued at or under $800 and that would otherwise qualify for the de minimis exemption will be subject to a duty rate of either 30% of their value or $25 per item. This will increase to $50 per item after June 1. This is in lieu of any other duties, including those imposed by prior orders.

The order is intended to not only address the proliferation of packages being sent to the U.S. without paying appropriate duties, but also deceptive shipping practices that have allowed the proliferation of synthetic opioids into the U.S.

PDD (NASDAQ:PDD), Alibaba (BABA), and JD.com (JD) shares were all down ~5%.

data.title: U.S. stock futures veer lower after hours as Trump outlines reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
U.S. stock index futures flipped lower in Wednesday's after-hours trading, as President Donald Trump outlined import tariffs on products coming worldwide into the U.S.

The minimum baseline tariff will be 10% and 49% is the highest tariff rate. Trump spoke at the White House's Rose Garden after day-session trading closed. As pre-announced, the U.S. will impose a 25% tariff on all foreign-made automobiles as of midnight.

Benchmark S&P 500 futures (SPX) fell -1.7%, and Nasdaq-100 futures (US100:IND) were -2.5%. Dow futures (INDU) shed -0.5%. Futures had popped up more than 1% as Trump's afternoon press event got underway.

Among the tariff plans, the U.S. will place a 34% levy rate on goods from China, a 24% rate for Japanese goods, a 20% rate on European Union products, and a 10% levy on U.K. and Brazilian products. 

Widely watched exchange-traded funds tracking key market indexes fell late Wednesday. The Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ) -3.3%, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY) -2.6%, and the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:VOO) -2.5%.

Canada and Mexico were absent from the White House's list of reciprocal tariffs. The U.S. dollar fell -0.9% against the Mexico's currency (USD:MXN), buying 20.172 pesos. The greenback also fell against Canada's currency (USD:CAD), down -0.5%. The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) -0.5%. 

Stocks closed the regular-session higher after spending most of the day in positive territory.

In the bond market, the the 10-year yield (US10Y) fell 5 basis points to 4.12%, while the shorter-end 2-year yield (US2Y) lost 5 basis points to 3.84%.

data.title: Bank stocks slide after tariffs loom over deal prospects
data.description:
Bank stocks dipped in Wednesday after-hours trading after President Donald Trump announced reciprocal tariffs against other countries that range from 10% to 49%.

While Trump said he expects that the tariffs will increase competition and bring down prices, in the short-term it's expected to increase prices for most imported goods. That could fuel inflation, which could lead the Federal Reserve to raise interest rates. As a result, higher interest rates would constrain M&A activity, as well as debt and equity issuance, services that bring in fees for banks.

JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) stock fell 2.4%, Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) stock slid 2.6%, Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) fell 2.7%, Citigroup (NYSE:C) dipped 2.5%, Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) declined 2.3%, and Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC) wilted 3.6%.

Large, regional bank stocks also shared in the pain. U.S. Bancorp (USB) -2.3%, Truist Financial (TFC) -1.8%, PNC Financial Services (PNC) -1.1%, Flagstar Financial (FLG) -2.0%, and Huntington Bancshares (HBAN) -2.7%.

Consumer finance stocks sank as well. Capital One Financial (COF) -3.3%, American Express (AXP) -2.1%, Synchrony Financial (SYF) -5.5%, Bread Financial Holdings (BFH) -3.1%, and Discover Financial Services (DFS) -4.0%. Auto lender Ally Financial (ALLY) dropped 1.6%.
